title: What Does “Normal” Look Like for the GCC After the Middle East Regional Conflict?
gist: Oxford Economics expects GCC economic activity to rebound within one to two years of a definitive end to the Middle East conflict, but with confidence and international capital flows lagging several quarters behind; tourism is the slowest sector while AI, diversification capex, and defense spending become new structural tailwinds.

insights.claim: Oxford Economics' base case is that GCC normalisation—a return to pre-conflict trends in growth, investment and capital flows—takes roughly one to two years from a definitive end to the Middle East conflict.
insights.speaker: Suki Masana
insights.so_what: Sets a concrete time horizon for re-entering GCC risk assets after a peace settlement.
insights.quote: Our base case is roughly one to two years from a definitive end to the conflict, though the path will be uneven across activity, confidence and capital.
insights.locator: By normalisation, we mean a return to pre-conflict trends

insights.claim: Day-to-day GCC activity such as cross-border trade and tourism should recover relatively quickly, with oil production normalising fast once the Strait of Hormuz fully opens and manufacturing and construction rebounding sharply but in staggered fashion.
insights.speaker: Suki Masana
insights.so_what: Real activity leads the recovery, so production and trade volumes rebound before sentiment-driven assets do.
insights.quote: oil production will normalise quickly once the Strait of Hormuz fully opens

insights.claim: Confidence and capital lag the real economy: capital flows typically trail by several quarters once a clear political settlement is in place, and absent that clarity risk premiums stay elevated even as activity normalises.
insights.speaker: Suki Masana
insights.so_what: Expect elevated risk premiums and weak inflows to persist for quarters after activity recovers; sentiment is the lagging trade.
insights.quote: Capital flows typically lag the real economy by several quarters once a clear political settlement is in place; absent that clarity, risk premiums stay elevated even as activity normalises.

insights.claim: GCC tourism arrivals are tracking a roughly 39% decline this year, followed by around 42% recovery in 2027 and a return to 2025 levels only by 2028, dragging tourism-dependent and non-oil growth.
insights.speaker: Suki Masana
insights.so_what: Tourism is the slowest-recovering sector; non-oil exposure to it is a multi-year drag worth underweighting.
insights.quote: Tourism arrivals are tracking a roughly 39% decline this year... followed by around 42% recovery in 2027 and a return to 2025 levels by 2028.
insights.locator: Some sectors, however, are likely to experience a longer recovery tail

insights.claim: Saudi Arabia and the UAE retain significant fiscal space and sovereign wealth firepower to smooth the cycle through public investment and are expected to deploy it to support domestic activity.
insights.speaker: Suki Masana
insights.so_what: SWF and fiscal countercyclical spending cushions Gulf downside; favors domestically-oriented Saudi/UAE assets.
insights.quote: Saudi Arabia and the UAE still have significant fiscal space and sovereign wealth firepower to smooth the cycle through public investment.
insights.locator: Saudi Arabia and the UAE still have significant fiscal space

insights.claim: The conflict leaves structural echoes in the GCC: higher risk premiums on capital, higher insurance and logistics costs, the need to diversify trade routes, and a demographic risk that wages may have to rise to attract expat talent.
insights.speaker: Suki Masana
insights.so_what: Permanently higher cost-of-doing-business factors erode the Gulf's pre-conflict margin profile even after normalization.
insights.quote: higher risk premiums on capital, higher insurance and logistics costs, and the need to diversify trade routes will weigh on confidence and the cost of doing business
insights.locator: A cyclical shock with structural echoes

insights.claim: The GCC remains well-positioned on AI thanks to low-cost power, capital to deploy, and willingness to move quickly on data-centre infrastructure.
insights.speaker: Suki Masana
insights.so_what: AI/data-centre buildout is a fresh growth driver for the Gulf; watch power and infrastructure plays tied to the region.
insights.quote: the GCC remains particularly well-positioned still, with low-cost power, capital to deploy and a willingness to move quickly on data-centre infrastructure

insights.claim: Higher public and SWF investment to reduce import dependence, improve food and water security, and scale back Strait of Hormuz dependency via pipelines, ports and rail, plus higher defence spending, could all become additional GCC growth tailwinds.
insights.speaker: Suki Masana
insights.so_what: Diversification and defense capex create durable demand for logistics, infrastructure and defense contractors in the Gulf.
insights.quote: Higher public and SWF investment in local diversification... pipelines, ports, rail networks—could all become additional tailwinds. Higher defence spending will add to growth too.
